title: Reward Model Ensembles Help Mitigate Overoptimization
abstract: This paper investigates whether combining ensembles with conservative optimization can mitigate overoptimization in RLHF for language models. The authors found that ensemble-based conservative optimization not only mitigates overoptimization but also improves performance.

introduction.topic: Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) for language models
introduction.problem: Overoptimization - a phenomenon where policy optimization appears to make progress according to the learned reward model but regresses with respect to the true reward function
introduction.previous_work: Gao et al. (2023) is the only work that studies overoptimization systematically, using a synthetic setup with a 'gold' reward model
introduction.research_question: Whether combining ensembles with conservative optimization can help mitigate overoptimization

methodology.setup: Identical to Gao et al. (2023) with one modification: the addition of 25% label noise to better reflect real-world RLHF conditions
methodology.approach: Using ensembles of reward models with conservative optimization

results.main_finding: Ensemble-based conservative optimization methods are effective in mitigating overoptimization and improving performance
results.comparison: The approach is relatively inexpensive compared to acquiring larger reward models
results.compatibility: The gains provided by the method are orthogonal to the gains achieved by increasing the reward model size

key_contributions: First study of using ensembles to counter overoptimization in RLHF-based fine-tuning of language models
Ensemble-based conservative optimization eliminates overoptimization for BoN and results in up to 70% improvement in some cases
For PPO, ensemble-based conservative optimization typically outperforms single reward model optimization
related_work: Gao et al. (2023) which systematically studied overoptimization using a synthetic setup with a 'gold' reward model
key_findings: Ensemble-based conservative optimization helps mitigate overoptimization
The approach results in improved performance
The method is relatively inexpensive compared to acquiring larger reward models
The gains provided by the method are orthogonal to the gains achieved by increasing the reward model size
